content.text: Schema-Driven I/O

Schema-driven I/O means defining your data shapes once — in Pydantic models — and deriving everything else from them: tool input schemas for Claude, FastAPI request/response types, OpenAPI documentation, and database DDL. This single-source-of-truth approach eliminates the drift that accumulates when schemas are written separately in multiple places.

attrs.alt: Schema-Driven I/O — Single Source of Truth

content.text: Defining the Canonical Schema
Start with a Pydantic BaseModel that captures the full data contract. Field descriptions and constraints become tool documentation that the model reads when deciding how to call the tool.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal
from datetime import date

class CreateTaskInput(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=200, description="Short task title")
    description: str = Field(
        default="",
        max_length=2000,
        description="Optional detailed description",
    )
    priority: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"] = Field(
        default="medium",
        description="Task urgency level",
    )
    due_date: date | None = Field(
        default=None,
        description="ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) or null if no deadline",
    )
    assignee_id: str | None = Field(
        default=None,
        description="User ID to assign the task to, or null",
    )
    tags: list[str] = Field(
        default_factory=list,
        description="Optional labels for filtering",
    )

class CreateTaskOutput(BaseModel):
    task_id: str
    title: str
    created_at: str   # ISO 8601 datetime
    url: str
Adding
description

to every Field is not documentation boilerplate — it is the text the model reads when deciding which arguments to pass to the tool. Precise descriptions produce significantly more accurate tool calls.
Deriving the Tool Schema from Pydantic
Use Pydantic's
model_json_schema()
to generate the tool input schema automatically. This guarantees the tool definition and the validation model never diverge.
When Pydantic generates the JSON schema, it includes
enum
constraints from
Literal
types and
minimum
maximum
from numeric field constraints. These appear in the tool schema the model receives, guiding it to produce valid values.
FastAPI Integration
The same Pydantic models serve as FastAPI request/response types, giving you automatic request validation and OpenAPI schema generation with zero extra code.

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import ValidationError

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/tasks", response_model=CreateTaskOutput)
async def create_task(body: CreateTaskInput) -> CreateTaskOutput:
    # FastAPI validates body against CreateTaskInput automatically.
    # Any constraint violation returns a 422 before this code runs.
    task_id = await db_insert_task(body)
    return CreateTaskOutput(
        task_id=task_id,
        title=body.title,
        created_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        url=f"https://app.example.com/tasks/{task_id}",
    )

@app.post("/agent/create-task")
async def agent_create_task(body: dict):
    try:
        parsed = run_task_creation_agent(body.get("request", ""))
    except ValueError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail=str(exc))
    task_id = await db_insert_task(parsed)
    return CreateTaskOutput(
        task_id=task_id,
        title=parsed.title,
        created_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        url=f"https://app.example.com/tasks/{task_id}",
    )
Both routes use the same
CreateTaskInput
model, so a change to the schema — adding a field, tightening a constraint — takes effect simultaneously in API validation, OpenAPI docs, and the tool call schema.
Schema Versioning

As the application evolves, tool schemas change. The model may have been called with an older schema that does not include a new required field. Handle this with versioned schemas and a migration layer.
Versioned schemas are particularly important for agentic workflows where tool calls are logged and replayed. A log entry captured with V1 schema must still parse correctly after a V2 upgrade.
Generating Database DDL from Pydantic
For simple persistence requirements, derive the database schema directly from the Pydantic model rather than maintaining a separate migration file.

import asyncpg
from pydantic import BaseModel

PYTHON_TO_PG = {
    "str": "TEXT",
    "int": "INTEGER",
    "float": "REAL",
    "bool": "BOOLEAN",
    "date": "DATE",
    "NoneType": None,  # skip nullable markers
}

async def ensure_table(pool: asyncpg.Pool, table: str, model: type[BaseModel]) -> None:
    fields = model.model_fields
    columns = ["id TEXT PRIMARY KEY", "created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()"]
    for name, info in fields.items():
        annotation = info.annotation
        base_type = getattr(annotation, "__origin__", annotation)
        # Handle Optional[X] as X with NULL allowed
        args = getattr(annotation, "__args__", None)
        nullable = args is not None and type(None) in args
        pg_type = PYTHON_TO_PG.get(
            (args[0] if nullable and args else annotation).__name__,
            "TEXT",
        )
        null_clause = "" if nullable else " NOT NULL"
        default_raw = info.default
        default_clause = f" DEFAULT '{default_raw}'" if default_raw not in (None, ...) else ""
        columns.append(f"{name} {pg_type}{null_clause}{default_clause}")

    ddl = f"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS {table} ({', '.join(columns)})"
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        await conn.execute(ddl)
The DDL generation above handles simple types. For production use, consider SQLModel (which combines Pydantic and SQLAlchemy) or Alembic migrations for full DDL control and rollback support.
Schema-Driven I/O Checklist
Concern
How schema-driven approach handles it
Input validation
FastAPI validates request body against Pydantic model automatically
Tool call accuracy
model_json_schema() generates precise enum and constraint hints for Claude
Output serialisation
model_dump() converts Pydantic model to JSON-safe dict
API documentation
FastAPI generates OpenAPI spec from the same models
Drift prevention
Single definition — change one file, update everywhere
Schema versioning
Subclass old model, add migration layer for older log entries
Database schema
Derive DDL or use SQLModel to share the model with ORM
Common Pitfalls
Using raw dicts as tool schemas.
Writing the tool input_schema by hand and separately maintaining a Pydantic model for validation creates two sources of truth that drift apart over time.
Missing field descriptions.
Omitting Field(description=...) means the model receives no guidance about what a field represents. This is especially harmful for fields like assignee_id where the format is not self-evident.
Ignoring $defs in model_json_schema().

Pydantic emits $defs for reused sub-models. The Anthropic API does not support $defs — flatten them using model_json_schema(mode='serialization') or by calling model.model_rebuild() and then inlining the references.
Not versioning tool schemas.
Adding a required field to a tool schema breaks replayed log entries that do not include that field. Always make new fields optional with a default.
Handling Nested and Discriminated Schemas
Real-world tool schemas often have nested structures or multiple variants. Pydantic supports both with nested models and discriminated unions.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Annotated, Literal, Union

class TextContent(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["text"] = "text"
    text: str

class ImageContent(BaseModel):
    type: Literal["image"] = "image"
    url: str
    alt_text: str = ""

Content = Annotated[
    Union[TextContent, ImageContent],
    Field(discriminator="type"),
]

class CreatePostInput(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=150)
    content_blocks: list[Content]
    published: bool = False

# Pydantic serialises the discriminated union correctly:
post = CreatePostInput(
    title="Hello World",
    content_blocks=[
        {"type": "text", "text": "Introduction paragraph."},
        {"type": "image", "url": "https://example.com/img.jpg"},
    ],
)
print(post.model_dump())
# {'title': 'Hello World', 'content_blocks': [{'type': 'text', 'text': '...'}, ...], ...}
Discriminated unions in the JSON schema become
oneOf
blocks with a constant
type
discriminator. Claude reads these correctly and constructs the right variant based on context.
Schema Introspection for Dynamic Toolsets

In multi-tenant or plugin-based architectures, different users may have different tools available. Generate tool definitions dynamically from a registry of Pydantic models rather than hardcoding them.
Round-Trip Validation

After the model calls a tool, round-trip validate the output through Pydantic before storing it. This catches cases where the model fills in values that pass JSON schema validation but violate business rules.
Business rules encoded as
@model_validator
methods run automatically during
CreateTaskInput(**block.input)
. If a model_validator raises, the caller receives a
ValidationError
— the same as a schema violation — and the retry loop feeds the error back to the model for correction.
Testing Schema-Driven Code
Because the schema is the single source of truth, tests focus on schema coverage: valid payloads, boundary values, and invalid inputs that should be rejected.

import pytest
from pydantic import ValidationError
from datetime import date, timedelta

def test_valid_task():
    task = CreateTaskInput(
        title="Fix login bug",
        priority="high",
        due_date=date.today() + timedelta(days=7),
    )
    assert task.title == "Fix login bug"

def test_title_too_long():
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError) as exc:
        CreateTaskInput(title="x" * 201)
    errors = exc.value.errors()
    assert any("max_length" in str(e) for e in errors)

def test_past_due_date_rejected():
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError) as exc:
        CreateTaskInput(title="Task", due_date=date(2020, 1, 1))
    assert "past" in str(exc.value)

def test_critical_without_due_date():
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError) as exc:
        CreateTaskInput(title="Urgent", priority="critical")
    assert "due_date" in str(exc.value)

def test_tool_schema_has_descriptions():
    schema = CreateTaskInput.model_json_schema()
    props = schema.get("properties", {})
    for field_name, info in CreateTaskInput.model_fields.items():
        if info.description:
            assert props[field_name].get("description"), \
                f"Field {field_name!r} description missing from JSON schema"
The last test — checking that descriptions survive into the JSON schema — prevents the common mistake of adding
description
to a sub-model field that Pydantic's schema generation happens to drop.
Summary
Schema-driven I/O centralises data definitions in
Pydantic models
and derives tool schemas, FastAPI validation, OpenAPI docs, and serialisation from a single source. Use
model_json_schema()
to generate tool input schemas automatically,
response_model=
for FastAPI output validation, and version your schemas with subclassing and migration layers to handle evolving tool contracts.
